metadata.lessonTitle: Aircraft Wiring Practices, Termination & Harness Fabrication

questions.learningObjective: Strain relief prevents tension transfer to connector pins or solder joints
questions.fullObjective: Explain the main reason for using strain relief in aircraft wiring: to prevent tension on the wire from being transferred to the connector pins or solder joints, which could cause intermittent connections or failures.

flashcard.definition: A strain relief is a clamping or support device attached near a connector that absorbs mechanical tension on the wire, preventing that tension from being transmitted to the connector pins or solder joints.
flashcard.analogy: Strain relief is like a strain relief on a phone charger cord — without it, every tug on the cable pulls directly on the connector pins and eventually breaks the joint.

jeopardy.question: This wiring feature prevents mechanical tension on a wire from being transferred to connector pins or solder joints, reducing the risk of intermittent connections.
choices: Strain relief
Backshell
Heat shrink tubing
Connector lock ring
jeopardy.explanation: Strain relief clamps the wire near the connector so any pulling force is absorbed by the clamp rather than transmitted to the pins or solder joints — preventing intermittent faults.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of strain relief in an aircraft wiring installation?
choices: To provide electrical insulation at connector entry points
To prevent tension on the wire from transferring to connector pins or solder joints
To seal the connector against moisture entry at the wire entry port
To ensure the wire maintains its minimum bend radius inside the connector
multipleChoice.explanation: Strain relief absorbs mechanical tension, ensuring that movement of the wire does not pull on the connector pins or solder joints — the primary cause of intermittent connection failures.

practice.question: During a wiggle test, an avionics technician finds that a nav radio squelch intermittently breaks when the antenna cable connector is flexed. On disassembly, the center pin appears loose. What is the most likely root cause?
choices: The antenna cable was too short, causing constant tension on the connector
The connector was not the correct type for the antenna impedance
Inadequate strain relief allowed tension on the cable to transfer to the center pin, eventually loosening the pin solder or crimp joint
The coaxial cable outer shield was making contact with the inner conductor
practice.explanation: Without proper strain relief, cable movement transfers tension directly to the center pin. Over time this loosens the connection, producing the intermittent symptom that appears during flex testing.

preassessment.question: What does strain relief prevent in an aircraft wiring installation?
choices: Moisture ingress
Connector corrosion
Excessive bend radius
Tension transfer to pins and joints

questions.learningObjective: Properly sized crimped terminals with calibrated ratcheting tool are preferred
questions.fullObjective: Select properly sized, type-approved crimped terminals applied with a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool as the most appropriate method for terminating aircraft wiring.

flashcard.definition: Aircraft wire termination requires type-approved, correctly sized crimp terminals applied with a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool — the ratcheting mechanism ensures the crimp cycle completes fully before releasing, guaranteeing a consistent, gas-tight connection.
flashcard.analogy: A calibrated ratcheting crimp tool is like a safety syringe that locks until the plunger fully depresses — you cannot release it halfway, ensuring every crimp is fully completed.

jeopardy.question: For aircraft wire termination, the preferred tool is this type of crimp tool because it cannot be released until the crimp cycle is fully completed, ensuring a consistent connection.
choices: Non-ratcheting pliers-type crimp tool
Calibrated ratcheting crimp tool
Soldering iron with rosin-core solder
Hex driver with compression insert
jeopardy.explanation: A calibrated ratcheting crimp tool locks at the correct crimp force and cannot be released prematurely, ensuring full crimp completion every time — the FAA-preferred method for aircraft wiring.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most appropriate method for terminating aircraft wiring?
choices: Solder the wire directly to the terminal using acid-core solder for a stronger bond
Use automotive-grade terminals with a standard pliers-type crimp tool
Use properly sized, type-approved crimped terminals installed with a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool
Wrap the wire around the terminal and secure with electrical tape
multipleChoice.explanation: The FAA-approved method for aircraft wire termination is type-approved crimp terminals with a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool — providing consistent, gas-tight, mechanically secure connections.

practice.question: A technician is terminating 22 AWG wires to a multi-pin connector during an avionics installation. Which approach best meets aviation maintenance standards?
choices: Solder each wire to its pin to ensure the best electrical connection
Use automotive push-on terminals for speed, then cover with heat shrink
Use any available crimp tool as long as the contact feels secure after crimping
Select the correct crimp contact for 22 AWG wire and insert it using a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool set to the manufacturer's specification
practice.explanation: The correct method: size-appropriate type-approved contacts, calibrated ratcheting crimp tool. This ensures gas-tight crimps that meet aviation standards.

preassessment.question: What type of crimp tool is required for aircraft wire termination?
choices: Calibrated ratcheting crimp tool
Non-ratcheting pliers
Standard automotive crimper
Soldering iron

questions.learningObjective: Connector function: reliable removable electrical junction for maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the function of a connector in aircraft wiring systems is to provide a reliable, removable electrical junction between wire harnesses and avionics equipment for ease of installation and maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Aircraft connectors provide a reliable, removable electrical junction between wire harnesses and avionics equipment — allowing LRUs to be disconnected and replaced without disturbing the aircraft wiring harness.
flashcard.analogy: A connector is like a wall outlet — it provides a reliable connection that can be disconnected and reconnected repeatedly without disturbing the wiring inside the wall.

jeopardy.question: The primary function of connectors in aircraft wiring is to provide reliable electrical junctions between harnesses and avionics units that can be disconnected for this purpose.
choices: Higher current carrying capacity
Reduced electromagnetic emissions
Ease of installation and maintenance (removable junction)
Increased wire bundle rigidity
jeopardy.explanation: Connectors allow LRUs to be removed and replaced without re-wiring the aircraft. This removability is the key function — reliable, repeatable electrical junctions for ease of maintenance.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of connectors in an aircraft wiring system?
choices: To increase the current-carrying capacity of the wiring harness
To reduce the total wire weight by eliminating unnecessary conductors
To provide mechanical rigidity to prevent wire bundle movement during flight
To provide reliable, removable electrical junctions between wire harnesses and avionics equipment for ease of installation and maintenance
multipleChoice.explanation: Connectors provide removable junctions — LRUs can be swapped without re-wiring. This maintainability is the core purpose of connectors in avionics systems.

practice.question: A newly hired avionics technician asks why there are so many connectors in an avionics installation rather than simply splicing all wires together. What is the most accurate explanation?
choices: Connectors provide removable, reliable junctions — allowing each LRU to be disconnected and replaced without cutting or re-terminating the aircraft harness
Connectors are required by FAA regulations to eliminate the use of wire splices in avionics
Connectors reduce current flow resistance because they are made of high-conductivity alloys
Connectors are used exclusively for their mechanical rigidity in high-vibration areas
practice.explanation: The maintenance advantage of connectors is removability: LRUs can be swapped quickly without affecting the harness. This drives the extensive use of connectors in avionics systems.

preassessment.question: Why are connectors used in aircraft wiring instead of direct splices?
choices: Higher current capacity
Reliable removable junctions for maintenance
Lower electrical resistance
Reduced wire weight

questions.learningObjective: Solder joints: heat the work before applying solder
questions.fullObjective: State the critical step in making a good solder joint: heat the work (wire and terminal) before applying solder so the solder flows into the joint, not onto a cold surface.

flashcard.definition: For a proper solder joint, the iron must heat the WORK (wire and terminal) — not the solder. Solder is then applied to the heated joint, flowing into all gaps. Applying solder to the iron creates a cold joint.
flashcard.analogy: Soldering correctly is like heating a pan before adding oil — if the pan is cold, the oil sits on the surface. If you heat the pan first, it properly integrates. Heat the work, then add solder.

jeopardy.question: The critical step in making a reliable solder joint is to heat this — not the solder — so the solder flows into the joint rather than balling up on a cold surface.
choices: The soldering iron tip only
The heat-shrink tubing adjacent to the joint
The solder wire only before touching the joint
The work (wire and terminal)
jeopardy.explanation: Heating the work (wire + terminal) ensures solder wets into the joint properly. Applying solder directly to the iron's tip produces cold joints that look shiny but have poor mechanical and electrical integrity.

multipleChoice.question: What is the critical technique for making a good solder joint in avionics work?
choices: Heat the work (both the wire and the terminal) with the iron, then apply solder to the heated work so it flows into the joint
Apply solder to the tip of the iron, then touch the iron to the joint to transfer the solder
Apply solder first, then heat the joint until the solder melts and flows
Tin only the iron tip, not the wire, to prevent bridging between adjacent pins
multipleChoice.explanation: The fundamental rule: heat the work first, not the solder. Solder applied to hot work wets properly and flows into all gaps. Solder applied to the iron creates cold joints.

practice.question: A technician is training an apprentice in avionics wire soldering. The apprentice touches solder to the iron tip, melts it, then touches the liquid solder blob to the terminal. What is wrong with this technique?
choices: The iron temperature is too high — solder should never be applied to a hot iron

The apprentice created a cold joint — applying solder to the iron instead of the work means the joint itself was never heated to the correct temperature, resulting in poor mechanical and electrical bonding
This technique is acceptable as long as adequate solder is applied
The terminal should be pre-tinned before applying solder via the iron tip
practice.explanation: Touching solder to the iron then transferring creates cold joints — the work (wire/terminal) was never heated to solder's flow temperature. The joint looks filled but has poor adhesion and high resistance.

preassessment.question: What must be heated first when making a solder joint?
choices: The solder wire
The iron tip only
The work (wire and terminal)
The heat-shrink sleeve

questions.learningObjective: PTFE/MIL-W-22759 is most common wire insulation in GA aircraft
questions.fullObjective: State that PTFE (Teflon) or MIL-W-22759 series wire is the most common type of wire insulation used in modern general aviation aircraft for its light weight, temperature resistance, and durability.

flashcard.definition: PTFE (polytetrafluoroethylene, brand name Teflon) insulated wire, designated MIL-W-22759 series, is the most common wire insulation in modern GA aircraft. It offers excellent temperature resistance (-65°C to +200°C), light weight, low dielectric loss, and chemical/abrasion resistance.
flashcard.analogy: PTFE wire is the non-stick pan of aircraft wiring — nothing sticks to it (chemicals slide off), it handles high heat, and it's durable under harsh conditions.

jeopardy.question: This military-specification wire, using PTFE (Teflon) insulation, is the most common choice in modern GA aircraft for its light weight, high temperature rating, and resistance to chemicals and abrasion.
choices: MIL-W-22759 (PTFE/Teflon insulated wire)
MIL-W-5086 (PVC insulated wire)
MIL-C-17 (coaxial cable)
MIL-W-16878 (Nylon insulated wire)
jeopardy.explanation: MIL-W-22759 PTFE (Teflon) wire is the dominant wire type in modern GA aircraft. Its combination of light weight, high temperature rating, chemical resistance, and durability makes it the standard.

multipleChoice.question: What type of wire insulation is most commonly used in modern general aviation aircraft?
choices: PVC (polyvinyl chloride) insulation — low cost and widely available
PTFE (Teflon) or MIL-W-22759 series — light weight, temperature resistant, and durable
Rubber insulation — flexible and impact resistant
Fiberglass insulation — used for highest temperature applications only
multipleChoice.explanation: PTFE (Teflon) wire per MIL-W-22759 is the industry standard for modern GA aircraft. PVC and rubber are too heavy and have limited temperature ranges. Fiberglass is for extreme heat zones only.

practice.question: A shop is ordering replacement wire for an avionics harness repair. The AMM calls for 'MIL-W-22759/16, 20 AWG.' A supply clerk asks what type of insulation this is. What is the correct answer?
choices: PVC insulation — the standard aircraft wire for general use
Fiberglass braid over silicone — for high-temperature engine bay use
PTFE (Teflon) insulation — the MIL-W-22759 series uses PTFE, which provides high temperature resistance and light weight
Nylon jacket over PVC — for use in pressurized cabin environments
practice.explanation: MIL-W-22759 designates PTFE (Teflon) insulated wire. The /16 suffix specifies the specific construction variant. PTFE wire is the standard for modern avionics harnesses.

preassessment.question: What insulation material does MIL-W-22759 wire use?
choices: PVC
Fiberglass
Rubber
PTFE (Teflon)

questions.learningObjective: Wiggle test detects intermittent connections not found in static testing
questions.fullObjective: Explain the purpose of a wiggle test near connectors before finalizing an installation: to detect intermittent connections that would not appear under static testing but could fail in service.

flashcard.definition: A wiggle test involves gently flexing wiring near connectors and terminal points while monitoring for circuit continuity changes; it detects intermittent connections from marginal crimps, loose pins, or strained solder joints that static testing would miss.
flashcard.analogy: A wiggle test is like gently shaking a table lamp to see if the bulb flickers — the static test said the bulb worked, but only movement reveals the loose socket connection.

jeopardy.question: Before finalizing an avionics installation, this test involves gently flexing wires near connectors to detect intermittent connections that static continuity tests would not find.
choices: Continuity test
Wiggle test
Megohm insulation test
Bus voltage measurement
jeopardy.explanation: The wiggle test simulates in-service wire movement. Intermittent connections from loose pins or marginal crimps will show up as momentary circuit breaks during the flex test.

multipleChoice.question: Why should a wiggle test be performed near connectors before finalizing an avionics installation?
choices: To verify that the connector locking mechanism clicks properly
To check that the connector backshell is correctly torqued to prevent rotation
To detect intermittent connections that would not be revealed by static continuity tests but could fail in flight
To ensure the wire insulation has not been damaged during installation
multipleChoice.explanation: Static tests pass connections that are marginally crimped or soldered. Dynamic flex (wiggle test) simulates service conditions — loose pins or marginal joints will break intermittently when the wire is flexed.

practice.question: An avionics installation passes all continuity and resistance checks on the bench. How should the technician verify the installation before the aircraft returns to service?
choices: The bench tests are sufficient — no additional checks are required
Inspect each connector visually under magnification for signs of damaged pins
Run the avionics for 30 minutes to allow thermal expansion to reveal loose connections
Perform a wiggle test near all connectors while monitoring circuit operation, to catch intermittent connections that static tests cannot detect
practice.explanation: Static tests cannot detect intermittent connections. The wiggle test dynamically flexes wires near connectors — the same stress that occurs in service — revealing marginal connections before the aircraft flies.

preassessment.question: What type of fault does a wiggle test detect that static testing cannot?
choices: Intermittent connections
Open circuits
Short circuits
High resistance

questions.learningObjective: Backshells provide mechanical strain relief and protect terminations
questions.fullObjective: Explain that backshells on aircraft connectors are used primarily to provide mechanical strain relief, protect the conductor terminations, and maintain connector integrity against vibration and moisture.

flashcard.definition: A backshell is the rear shell of an aircraft connector that clamps the wire bundle, providing mechanical strain relief, protecting the terminations from vibration damage, and (in environmentally sealed types) excluding moisture and contaminants.
flashcard.analogy: A backshell is like the rubber boot on a garden hose fitting — it clamps the hose, prevents the hose from pulling the connection apart, and keeps water from entering the fitting mechanism.

jeopardy.question: This component on the rear of an aircraft connector clamps the wire bundle to provide strain relief, protect pin terminations from vibration, and (in sealed types) exclude moisture.
choices: Connector coupling ring
Connector insert retention clip
Backshell
Outer shell ground band
jeopardy.explanation: The backshell serves three functions: mechanical strain relief (clamps the bundle), protection of pin terminations (against vibration), and environmental sealing against moisture and contaminants.

multipleChoice.question: What are the primary purposes of a backshell on an aircraft connector?
choices: To increase the connector's current-carrying capacity and reduce contact resistance
To color-code the connector for identification and reduce installation errors
To provide an electrical ground path from the connector shell to the wire bundle
To provide mechanical strain relief, protect terminations from vibration, and maintain environmental integrity
multipleChoice.explanation: Backshells provide: (1) strain relief — clamp the bundle so tension doesn't pull on pins, (2) vibration protection — shield pin terminations from dynamic loads, and (3) environmental sealing in sealed variants.

practice.question: During inspection, a technician finds a multi-pin connector with no backshell installed. The wire bundle enters the connector housing unsupported. What risk does this present?
choices: Without the backshell, wire tension and vibration transfer directly to the pin terminations — increasing risk of intermittent connections, pin damage, or complete circuit failure
The connector cannot be mated without a backshell — it will always show a continuity fault
The missing backshell allows the outer shell to rotate, which increases contact resistance
Without the backshell, electromagnetic shielding of the connector is compromised
practice.explanation: Without a backshell, there is no strain relief or vibration protection. Wire movement and vibration are transmitted directly to the crimp or solder joints on each pin — a primary cause of intermittent faults.

preassessment.question: What does a connector backshell primarily provide?
choices: Color coding for identification
Mechanical strain relief and termination protection
Increased current capacity
Electrical ground path

questions.learningObjective: Wire routing: keep away from heat, moving parts, sharp edges, fluid lines
questions.fullObjective: State that the most important consideration for wire routing in an aircraft installation is keeping wires away from heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and fuel or fluid lines to prevent chafing and damage.

flashcard.definition: The most important wire routing consideration is separation from: heat sources (exhaust, heating ducts), moving parts (cables, actuators), sharp edges (structural members, brackets), and fluid lines (fuel, hydraulic, oil) — all of which can damage insulation and cause failures.
flashcard.analogy: Wire routing is like routing plumbing away from a hot water heater, moving machinery, sharp corners, and electrical panels — common sense separation prevents damage from the most obvious hazards.

jeopardy.question: When routing an aircraft wire bundle, it must be kept away from these four hazard types to prevent chafing and insulation damage: heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and these.
choices: Pressurized air lines
Navigation antenna cables
Oxygen system plumbing
Fuel and fluid lines
jeopardy.explanation: The four wire routing hazard categories are: heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and fuel/fluid lines. Fluid line leaks combined with damaged wire insulation can create fire or short circuit hazards.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most important consideration when routing wire bundles in an aircraft?
choices: Routing wires away from heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and fuel/fluid lines to prevent chafing and damage
Keeping wire bundles as short as possible to reduce weight
Routing all wire bundles along the same path to simplify inspection
Ensuring all wires run parallel to the fuselage centerline
multipleChoice.explanation: The four hazards — heat, moving parts, sharp edges, and fluid lines — can all damage wire insulation and cause shorts, opens, or fire. Routing away from these hazards is the primary wire routing requirement.

practice.question: A technician is routing a new ARINC 429 signal cable from the avionics bay to the aft cabin. The most direct path passes over the fuel selector valve and near the rudder cable. What should the technician do?
choices: Use the direct path to minimize wire length and reduce resistance
Select an alternate routing away from the fuel selector (fluid line hazard) and the rudder cable (moving part hazard), using the approved alternate path from the installation manual
Add extra heat-shrink tubing over the cable to protect it from the rudder cable contact
Route directly over the fuel valve but add a protective conduit only over the rudder cable section
practice.explanation: The direct path crosses a fuel line and a moving part — both prohibited routing hazards. An alternate approved route that avoids both hazards is required.

preassessment.question: What must wire bundles be kept away from when routed through an aircraft?
choices: Only heat sources
Only moving parts and sharp edges
Heat, moving parts, sharp edges, and fuel/fluid lines
Only fuel and fluid lines

questions.learningObjective: Select wire gauge using wire load analysis and ampacity tables
questions.fullObjective: Apply the critical step of consulting the aircraft's wire load analysis and the manufacturer's ampacity tables to select the correct wire gauge based on current load, wire run length, and temperature environment.

flashcard.definition: Correct wire gauge selection requires consulting the wire load analysis and manufacturer's ampacity tables, considering: circuit current draw, wire run length (voltage drop), and installation temperature environment — not just the current rating alone.
flashcard.analogy: Choosing wire gauge is like sizing a pipe for water flow — you need to account for flow rate (current), pipe length (voltage drop), and temperature (affects flow resistance). One factor alone gives the wrong size.

jeopardy.question: To select the correct wire gauge for an aircraft installation, a technician must consult the wire load analysis and manufacturer's ampacity tables, considering current load, run length, and this factor.
choices: Temperature environment of the installation area
Wire color and labeling requirements
Number of connectors in the circuit
ARINC 429 bus loading percentage
jeopardy.explanation: Wire gauge selection depends on three factors: current load (ampacity), wire run length (voltage drop), and temperature environment (de-rating of ampacity in high-temp zones). All three must be considered.

multipleChoice.question: How should an avionics technician select the correct wire gauge for a new circuit?
choices: Use the smallest wire that fits the connector contact to minimize weight
Consult the aircraft's wire load analysis and manufacturer's ampacity tables, considering current load, run length, and installation temperature
Match the wire gauge to whatever gauge was used for adjacent circuits in the harness
Use 20 AWG for all avionics circuits as the standard aviation wire size
multipleChoice.explanation: Wire gauge selection is a calculated decision — ampacity tables give the base rating, then de-rate for temperature and check voltage drop for the run length. The aircraft's wire load analysis documents this for each circuit.

practice.question: A technician is adding a new 5-amp avionics circuit with a 12-foot wire run in an area near the engine firewall where temperatures can reach 85°C. How should the wire gauge be determined?
choices: Choose 20 AWG since it is rated for 5A and is standard for avionics circuits
Use the smallest wire that crimps into the available connector contacts
Consult the wire load analysis and ampacity tables for the wire type, de-rate for the 85°C ambient temperature, then verify voltage drop for the 12-foot run before selecting the gauge
Match the wire used in the adjacent electrical bus to maintain harness uniformity
practice.explanation: High ambient temperature requires de-rating ampacity. A 5A circuit at room temperature might require 22 AWG, but at 85°C the same wire may need to be 18 AWG after de-rating. Wire load analysis guides the correct selection.

preassessment.question: What three factors must be considered when selecting aircraft wire gauge?
choices: Color, length, and connector type
Bundle size, routing, and shield type
Insulation type, resistance, and capacitance
Current load, run length, and temperature environment

questions.learningObjective: EMI reduction: separate power/signal wires, use twisted pair or shielded cable
questions.fullObjective: Apply EMI reduction techniques when wiring a new aircraft lighting system, including separating power and signal wires and using twisted-pair or shielded cables where appropriate.

flashcard.definition: To reduce electromagnetic interference in aircraft wiring: separate power wires from signal wires (minimum 3-inch separation for most installations), use twisted-pair cables for differential signals, and use shielded cables for sensitive analog or digital signals near high-current wiring.
flashcard.analogy: Separating power and signal wires is like keeping your phone away from the microwave — the microwave radiates EMI that disrupts the phone's signal. Distance and shielding are the solutions.

jeopardy.question: When wiring an aircraft lighting system to prevent EMI in adjacent avionics, power wires should be separated from signal wires, and these cable types should be used for sensitive signals.
choices: Unshielded solid-core wire
Twisted-pair or shielded cables
High-voltage coaxial cables
Standard PVC automotive wire
jeopardy.explanation: The two key EMI reduction techniques are: (1) physical separation of power and signal wires, and (2) using twisted-pair or shielded cable for sensitive signals to reduce coupling from nearby power wiring.

multipleChoice.question: Which wiring practices reduce EMI in a new aircraft lighting installation near avionics?
choices: Route all wires in the same bundle to keep them organized and easy to inspect
Use the largest wire gauge available to reduce resistance and minimize voltage drop noise
Separate power and signal wires, and use twisted-pair or shielded cables for sensitive signal runs
Connect all shields to ground at both ends to ensure maximum noise reduction
multipleChoice.explanation: EMI reduction requires physical separation between power and signal wiring, plus twisted-pair (cancels magnetic field noise) or shielded cables (blocks capacitive coupling) for sensitive signal conductors.

practice.question: After installing a new high-current LED landing light system, the avionics technician notices nav radio reception quality has degraded. What EMI mitigation should be applied?
choices: Increase the nav radio volume to overcome the noise floor raised by the LED system
Install a larger circuit breaker on the landing light circuit to reduce current spikes
Replace the LED lights with incandescent lights which produce less EMI
Reroute the landing light power wiring away from the nav radio antenna feeder, and add shielded cable or a ferrite filter to the LED power leads
practice.explanation: High-current LED switching circuits can radiate broadband EMI. Separating the power wiring from the nav antenna feeder and adding shielding or ferrite suppression on the LED leads are the correct mitigation steps.

preassessment.question: What are the two primary EMI reduction techniques for aircraft wiring?
choices: Separating power/signal wires and using shielded/twisted pair
Larger wire gauge and direct grounding
Running all wires in conduit and using solid-core wire
Using coaxial cable for all circuits and minimizing wire length

questions.learningObjective: Wire bundle too large for routing path: consult manual for alternate route
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire bundle that does not fit the designated routing path, determine the correct action: consult the installation manual for an approved alternate route before forcing or trimming the bundle.

flashcard.definition: When a wire bundle does not fit a designated routing path, the correct action is to consult the installation manual for an approved alternate route — never force the bundle (damages insulation) or trim/omit wires (alters the certified design).
flashcard.analogy: If your luggage doesn't fit in the overhead bin, you don't force it or remove items — you check a bag using the airline's approved process. Same principle: use the approved alternate method.

jeopardy.question: When a wire bundle is too large for its designated routing path, the correct first action is to consult this document for an approved alternate routing solution.
choices: The aircraft logbook for previous maintenance entries
The shop supervisor to determine if the bundle can be forced in
The installation manual for an approved alternate route
The wire manufacturer's data sheet for a thinner equivalent
jeopardy.explanation: The installation manual is the authority for all routing decisions. An approved alternate route maintains airworthiness; forcing a bundle or trimming wires without approval compromises the certified design.

multipleChoice.question: A wire bundle is too large to fit through the designated routing path in the aircraft structure. What is the correct action?
choices: Force the bundle through the path by compressing it to fit — the harness will expand back to shape
Remove wires from the bundle to reduce its diameter, splicing them back in after routing
Trim the cable ties to allow the bundle to spread and route through multiple adjacent holes
Consult the installation manual for an approved alternate routing path before modifying the bundle or the structure
multipleChoice.explanation: Forcing a bundle can damage insulation; removing or rerouting wires without approval alters the certified design. The installation manual must be consulted for an approved alternate route.

practice.question: During installation of a new avionics harness, the technician finds the bundle is 30% too large for the designated firewall passthrough grommet. The installation manual does not address this situation. What should the technician do?
choices: Stop work and coordinate with the aircraft manufacturer or an FAA-approved avionics installer to obtain an approved alternate routing or modification
File the grommet opening larger to accommodate the bundle — the airframe material can support the modification
Split the bundle into two smaller bundles and route them through adjacent holes
Reduce wire gauges throughout the harness to make the bundle smaller
practice.explanation: If the manual doesn't address the situation, work must stop — modifying the structure or rerouting without approval requires coordination with the manufacturer or FAA-certified engineering to maintain airworthiness.

preassessment.question: What should be done when a wire bundle is too large for its designated path?
choices: Force it through
Consult the installation manual for an alternate route
Trim some wires out
File the hole larger

questions.learningObjective: Shield termination: ground at one end only to prevent ground loops
questions.fullObjective: Apply the accepted practice for shield termination: connect the shield drain wire to ground at one end only (typically the equipment end) to prevent ground loops that cause EMI.

flashcard.definition: The standard practice for cable shield termination is to ground the shield drain wire at one end only (typically the equipment/receiver end), leaving the other end floating — this prevents ground loops where differences in ground potential between two points create circulating current that radiates EMI.
flashcard.analogy: Grounding a shield at both ends creates a loop antenna — any AC difference between two ground points drives current through the loop, creating exactly the interference you wanted to prevent. Ground at one end: no loop, no noise.

jeopardy.question: The standard shield termination practice for avionics wiring grounds the drain wire at one end only to prevent this type of interference caused by potential differences between two ground points.
choices: Short circuit faults
Voltage spikes from circuit switching
Capacitive coupling at high frequencies
Ground loops causing EMI
jeopardy.explanation: Grounding a shield at both ends creates a ground loop — a closed conductor loop that acts as a transformer secondary. Any AC difference between the two ground points drives circulating current, creating EMI.

multipleChoice.question: Why should an aircraft cable shield be grounded at one end only?
choices: To prevent a ground loop — grounding both ends creates a conductive loop where potential differences between grounds circulate current and create EMI
To maximize shielding effectiveness by concentrating ground current at one point
To comply with ARINC 429 voltage specification requirements for shield termination
To allow the shield to float at one end for use as a second signal conductor
multipleChoice.explanation: Two ground points always have a small potential difference at AC frequencies. Grounding the shield at both ends forms a loop between the two grounds — current flows, creating a magnetic field that radiates EMI rather than rejecting it.

practice.question: After routing a new ARINC 429 signal cable with the shield grounded at both the avionics unit and the circuit breaker panel, a technician observes unexpected EMI on adjacent audio circuits. What is the likely cause?
choices: The ARINC 429 signal is too strong and is leaking through the shield
The shield grounded at both ends has created a ground loop — current flowing between the two ground points generates EMI that couples into adjacent circuits
The ARINC 429 cable is too close to the audio cable, causing direct capacitive coupling
The shield drain wire has insufficient cross-section to carry the return current effectively
practice.explanation: Grounding the shield at both ends is the textbook cause of ground loop EMI. The fix is to lift the shield ground at one end (typically leaving the source end floating) to break the loop.

preassessment.question: Where should an aircraft cable shield be grounded?
choices: Both ends
Neither end (floating)
One end only
Center of the run

questions.learningObjective: High-temperature wire required near aircraft engines
questions.fullObjective: State that high-temperature wire such as MIL-W-22759/16 (PTFE-insulated) or fiberglass-insulated wire is required in high-temperature areas near aircraft engines.

flashcard.definition: Near aircraft engines and other high-temperature zones, MIL-W-22759/16 (PTFE/Teflon-insulated, rated to ~200°C) or fiberglass-insulated wire is required; standard PVC or nylon-insulated wire cannot withstand engine bay temperatures.
flashcard.analogy: Using standard wire near an aircraft engine is like using a regular plastic bag next to an oven — it will melt. You need a heat-rated container (PTFE or fiberglass wire) for the heat zone.

jeopardy.question: In the high-temperature zone adjacent to an aircraft engine, wire insulation must be rated for elevated temperatures; PTFE (MIL-W-22759/16) or this type of insulation is typically required.
choices: Fiberglass-insulated wire
PVC insulation rated to 60°C
Nylon-jacketed wire
Standard rubber-insulated wire
jeopardy.explanation: Fiberglass-insulated wire handles temperatures up to 400°C+ and is used in the most extreme heat zones near engines. PTFE (MIL-W-22759) is used for moderate-high temperature zones.

multipleChoice.question: What type of wire insulation is required in high-temperature areas near aircraft engines?
choices: Standard PVC insulation rated to 60°C
High-temperature wire such as PTFE (MIL-W-22759/16) or fiberglass-insulated wire
Nylon-jacketed wire with a temperature rating of 80°C
Rubber-insulated wire with a braided cotton outer jacket
multipleChoice.explanation: Engine bay temperatures can exceed 150°C or more. Only high-temperature insulations (PTFE at ~200°C, fiberglass at 400°C+) are suitable. PVC, nylon, and rubber insulations would melt or degrade.

practice.question: A technician is routing a new fuel flow indicator wire through the engine compartment of a piston aircraft. The area reaches 180°C at maximum power. Which wire specification is correct?
choices: MIL-W-5086 PVC-insulated wire — standard aviation wire rated to 105°C
Automotive-grade wire with vinyl insulation rated to 90°C
MIL-W-22759/16 PTFE-insulated wire — rated to approximately 200°C for high-temperature environments
Standard 20 AWG appliance wire — the engine is air-cooled and temperatures are not critical
practice.explanation: At 180°C, only PTFE (MIL-W-22759/16, rated ~200°C) or fiberglass wire is appropriate. PVC, vinyl, and standard aviation wire would fail at these temperatures.

preassessment.question: What wire insulation is required near aircraft engines?
choices: PVC (60°C rated)
Rubber with cotton braid
Nylon (80°C rated)
PTFE or fiberglass (high temperature)

questions.learningObjective: Ratcheting crimp tool and contact insertion/extraction tools for connector work
questions.fullObjective: State that a ratcheting crimp tool and the appropriate contact insertion/extraction tools are common tools used to install contacts in aircraft wiring connectors.

flashcard.definition: Installing contacts in aircraft connectors requires two tool types: a ratcheting crimp tool (to crimp the contact onto the wire) and contact insertion/extraction tools (to seat the contact in the connector insert or remove it without damage).
flashcard.analogy: Like a two-step process: a crimper presses the bullet into the cartridge (contact onto wire), and a specialized tool loads the cartridge into the gun (contact into connector). Different tools for different tasks.

jeopardy.question: For installing contacts in aircraft multi-pin connectors, two specialized tools are required: a ratcheting crimp tool and these, which seat and remove contacts without damaging the connector insert.
choices: Needle-nose pliers and a pick set
Contact insertion and extraction tools
Torque wrench and anti-rotation tool
Soldering iron and desoldering wick
jeopardy.explanation: Contact insertion tools push the crimp contact into the connector insert until it locks; extraction tools release the retention clip to remove a contact. Using pliers or picks risks damaging contacts and inserts.

multipleChoice.question: What tools are required for installing contacts in aircraft wiring connectors?
choices: Soldering iron and needle-nose pliers
Non-ratcheting crimp tool and a flat-blade screwdriver
Ratcheting crimp tool and appropriate contact insertion/extraction tools
Heat gun and a knurled hex driver for the contact retention rings
multipleChoice.explanation: Proper connector work requires a calibrated ratcheting crimp tool (to terminate the wire to the contact) and the correct insertion/extraction tool set (to seat and remove contacts from the connector insert).

practice.question: A technician needs to replace a single damaged contact in a 25-pin D-sub connector. Which tools are needed?
choices: A soldering iron to desolder the damaged contact and re-solder the replacement
A heat gun to soften the connector insert and release the contact
Needle-nose pliers to pull out the damaged contact and push in the replacement
The correct extraction tool to remove the damaged contact, a ratcheting crimp tool to terminate the wire on the new contact, and the insertion tool to seat the new contact in the connector
practice.explanation: Contact replacement requires: extraction tool (releases the contact retention and removes it cleanly), ratcheting crimp tool (terminates the wire on the new contact), and insertion tool (seats the new contact correctly in the insert).

preassessment.question: What two tool types are needed for aircraft connector contact installation?
choices: Ratcheting crimp tool and insertion/extraction tools
Soldering iron and pliers
Heat gun and pick set
Hex driver and torque wrench

questions.learningObjective: Wire crimper creates gas-tight low-resistance connection by mechanical deformation
questions.fullObjective: Explain that a wire crimper mechanically deforms a connector terminal around a stripped wire end to create a gas-tight, low-resistance electrical and mechanical connection.

flashcard.definition: A crimp tool mechanically deforms the terminal barrel around wire strands under controlled force, creating a gas-tight zone where metal-to-metal contact occurs and oxidation cannot penetrate — resulting in a low-resistance, mechanically strong, permanent connection.
flashcard.analogy: Crimping is like pressing clay around a wire — the clay (terminal) deforms tightly around every strand, filling all gaps. Gas-tight means no air (no oxidation) can reach the wire-to-terminal interface.

jeopardy.question: A properly crimped aircraft wire terminal creates this type of connection by mechanically deforming metal tightly around wire strands, excluding air and preventing oxidation.
choices: A mechanically welded fusion bond
A soldered joint with rosin flux residue
A gas-tight, low-resistance electrical and mechanical connection
A press-fit thermal joint using heat-activated adhesive
jeopardy.explanation: The crimp process deforms the terminal barrel tightly around wire strands, creating gas-tight metal-to-metal contact. No air means no oxidation, producing a stable, low-resistance, permanently secure connection.

multipleChoice.question: How does a wire crimper create a reliable electrical connection?
choices: By heating the terminal until it flows around the wire, forming a solder-like bond
By pressing a spring-loaded clamp onto the wire to hold it in place with clamping force
By compressing the wire strands until they fuse with the terminal through friction welding
By mechanically deforming the terminal barrel around the stripped wire end to create a gas-tight, low-resistance electrical and mechanical connection
multipleChoice.explanation: Mechanical deformation of the terminal creates gas-tight metal-to-metal contact. Gas-tight means oxidation cannot penetrate the interface, ensuring the connection remains low resistance over the life of the aircraft.

practice.question: A student asks why properly crimped connections are preferred over solder for aircraft wire terminations. What is the most accurate technical explanation?
choices: A properly calibrated crimp creates a gas-tight connection — the terminal deforms tightly around wire strands, excluding air and preventing oxidation, resulting in a more consistent and vibration-resistant joint than solder
Crimped connections can be easily un-done and redone, while solder is permanent
Crimping is faster and less expensive than soldering, which is the primary reason for its use
Soldered connections are prohibited by FAA on all certificated aircraft
practice.explanation: Gas-tight mechanical deformation is the technical advantage of crimping over soldering: oxidation cannot reach the wire-metal interface, preventing the contact resistance increase that solder joints can develop over time under vibration.

preassessment.question: What type of connection does a properly crimped terminal create?
choices: Welded fusion bond
Gas-tight low-resistance connection
Solder-reinforced joint
Press-fit spring clamp

questions.learningObjective: Prohibited wiring practices: soldering crimp contacts, automotive connectors, unprotected wire ends
questions.fullObjective: List practices to avoid during aircraft wiring installation: soldering wires to contacts designed for crimping, using automotive-grade connectors, and leaving unterminated wire ends unprotected.

flashcard.definition: Three prohibited aircraft wiring practices: (1) soldering wires onto contacts designed for crimping — the solder can wick up the wire causing a stiff heat-affected zone, (2) using automotive-grade connectors — not rated or approved for aviation environments, (3) leaving unterminated wire ends unprotected — shorts, arcing, and corrosion hazard.
flashcard.analogy: These are aviation's equivalent of workplace safety violations — using the wrong tool (automotive parts), misapplying the right tool (soldering crimp contacts), and leaving a hazard unaddressed (open wire ends).

jeopardy.question: Three practices prohibited in aircraft wiring installations are: using automotive-grade connectors, leaving unterminated wire ends unprotected, and this.
choices: Using PTFE-insulated wire in ambient temperature areas
Using ratcheting crimp tools for contact installation
Installing heat-shrink tubing over connector backshells
Soldering wires to contacts designed for crimping
jeopardy.explanation: Soldering onto crimp contacts wicks solder up the wire, creating a brittle heat-affected zone at the solder line — a common point of wire fracture under vibration. Crimp contacts must be crimped, not soldered.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following is a prohibited aircraft wiring practice?
choices: Soldering wires to contacts designed for crimping, or using automotive-grade connectors not approved for aviation
Using PTFE wire rated MIL-W-22759 for avionics harnesses
Using a ratcheting crimp tool for all wire terminations
Performing a wiggle test after connector assembly to check for intermittents
multipleChoice.explanation: Prohibited practices include soldering onto crimp-only contacts (creates brittle solder wicking), using automotive connectors (not aviation-rated), and leaving unprotected wire ends. All approved methods — PTFE wire, ratcheting crimpers, wiggle tests — are acceptable.

practice.question: An apprentice technician, wanting to ensure a 'better' connection, applies solder to a wire after crimping it into a crimp-type connector contact. Why is this incorrect?
choices: Soldering is only prohibited in pressurized aircraft — it is acceptable in small GA aircraft

Adding solder to a crimp contact wicks solder up the wire insulation, creating a rigid zone that concentrates vibration stress at the solder line — a location very prone to wire breakage under vibration
Solder reduces the contact resistance of the crimp, which is desirable for signal integrity
Soldering crimp contacts is only prohibited when using rosin-core solder — acid-core solder is acceptable
practice.explanation: Solder wicks between wire strands, stiffening the wire and creating a rigid boundary between the soldered and flexible portions. Vibration stress concentrates at this boundary, causing fatigue fractures over time.

preassessment.question: Which practice is prohibited in aircraft wiring?
choices: Using PTFE wire
Performing wiggle tests
Soldering contacts designed for crimping
Using ratcheting crimp tools

questions.learningObjective: Shield floating at both ends provides no EMI protection
questions.fullObjective: Predict that a shielded wire with the shield floating at both ends will provide no EMI protection because there is no path for induced currents to flow to ground.

flashcard.definition: A shielded cable provides EMI protection only when the shield is connected to ground, creating a low-impedance path for induced interference currents to flow to ground rather than onto the signal wire. A floating shield (ungrounded at both ends) cannot divert induced currents and provides no practical shielding.
flashcard.analogy: A floating shield is like a security fence with no connection to the ground — charges can still build up on it, they just can't discharge safely. Without a ground path, the shield cannot do its job.

jeopardy.question: A shielded cable provides no EMI protection when the shield is floating (ungrounded) at both ends because induced interference currents have no path to flow here.
choices: To ground
Into the signal conductors
Through the outer jacket
Into the connector backshell
jeopardy.explanation: A grounded shield provides a low-impedance path to ground for induced currents. A floating shield cannot divert these currents — they have nowhere to go except into the signal conductors.

multipleChoice.question: An avionics technician installs a shielded cable but leaves both ends of the shield drain wire unconnected (floating). What will be the EMI performance?
choices: Maximum EMI protection — floating shields are theoretically perfect insulators
No EMI protection — induced interference currents have no path to ground and will couple onto the signal conductors
Partial EMI protection — one floating end still provides some capacitive shielding
Improved EMI protection — no ground means no ground loop noise
multipleChoice.explanation: A floating shield cannot divert induced interference currents because there is no ground path. Induced currents have nowhere to go except onto the signal conductors — the shield provides no protection.

practice.question: After a harness repair, a technician notices both ends of the shield drain wire were left unconnected because the original installation couldn't be determined. The system now shows EMI problems. What is the cause?
choices: The shield is too thick and is absorbing signal energy from the center conductor
The shield drain wires at both ends need to be connected together to form a short-circuit current path
Both ends of the shield are floating — induced currents have no path to ground, so the shield provides no EMI protection and interference couples directly onto the signal wires
The EMI is caused by the shield acting as an antenna because it is floating and not absorbing interference
practice.explanation: A floating shield provides no EMI protection. The drain wire must be grounded at one end (and only one end) to give induced currents a path to ground. With both ends floating, there's no shielding at all.

preassessment.question: What EMI protection does a shield floating at both ends provide?
choices: Full protection at all frequencies
Protection only above 1 MHz
Partial protection at DC only
No protection

questions.learningObjective: AWG: higher number = smaller wire
questions.fullObjective: State that wire gauge in aircraft wiring is the numerical designation of conductor cross-sectional area per the American Wire Gauge (AWG) system, where a higher number means a smaller wire.

flashcard.definition: The American Wire Gauge (AWG) system designates conductor size: larger AWG numbers = smaller wire diameters. Common avionics sizes: 22 AWG (signal), 20 AWG (medium current), 16 AWG (higher current). A 22 AWG wire is smaller than a 16 AWG wire.
flashcard.analogy: AWG is counterintuitive — like hotel room numbers that go DOWN as you get to the luxury suites. Smaller AWG number = larger, more expensive (more copper) wire.

jeopardy.question: In the AWG system, a 22 AWG wire is smaller in diameter than a 16 AWG wire because in this numbering system, higher numbers indicate smaller conductors.
choices: A 16 AWG wire is smaller because lower numbers indicate finer wire
A 22 AWG wire is smaller — higher AWG numbers mean smaller conductor cross-section
Both wires are the same size because AWG only specifies insulation thickness
A 22 AWG wire has a larger conductor but thinner insulation than 16 AWG
jeopardy.explanation: In AWG, higher number = smaller wire. 22 AWG < 16 AWG in diameter. This counterintuitive system is important to know when selecting wire for current-carrying capacity.

multipleChoice.question: In the American Wire Gauge (AWG) system, what does a higher gauge number indicate?
choices: A larger conductor cross-sectional area and higher current capacity
A higher temperature rating for the wire insulation
A smaller conductor cross-sectional area — the conductor is physically smaller
A heavier overall wire construction with more insulation layers
multipleChoice.explanation: Higher AWG number = smaller wire. 22 AWG has a smaller conductor than 16 AWG. This is essential when selecting wire for a specific current load — choosing too high a gauge number means an undersized wire.

practice.question: A maintenance manual specifies 20 AWG wire for a replacement circuit. The supply room only has 22 AWG on the shelf. Can the technician substitute 22 AWG for 20 AWG?
choices: Yes — 22 AWG is a larger wire than 20 AWG in the AWG system
No — different gauge numbers always require a maintenance manual deviation regardless of size
Yes — the AWG numbers are close enough that the difference is negligible for avionics
No — 22 AWG is SMALLER than 20 AWG in AWG (higher number = smaller wire); a smaller wire may overheat on a circuit designed for 20 AWG current levels
practice.explanation: 22 AWG is smaller than 20 AWG. Using a smaller wire on a circuit rated for 20 AWG means the wire may carry more current than its ampacity allows, creating an overheating and fire risk.

preassessment.question: In the AWG system, does a higher number mean larger or smaller wire?
choices: Smaller wire
Larger wire
Larger insulation, smaller conductor
No difference — just temperature rating

questions.learningObjective: Shielded wire prevents EMI coupling into and emission from sensitive signal wires
questions.fullObjective: Explain that shielded wire is used in avionics systems primarily to prevent electromagnetic interference from coupling into sensitive signal wires and to prevent signal emissions from interfering with other systems.

flashcard.definition: Shielded wire provides two-way EMI protection: (1) incoming — the conductive shield prevents external electromagnetic fields from coupling onto the signal conductor; (2) outgoing — the shield contains signal emissions and prevents them from interfering with adjacent systems.
flashcard.analogy: A shielded cable is like a room with soundproofing — it keeps outside noise from coming in AND keeps inside noise from going out. Both directions are protected.

jeopardy.question: Shielded wire in avionics systems prevents external EMI from coupling in AND prevents signal emissions from doing this.
choices: Grounding through the connector backshell
Reducing cable capacitance
Interfering with other systems
Creating voltage drops along the run
jeopardy.explanation: Shielded wire provides bi-directional EMI protection: it blocks external fields from reaching the signal conductor AND contains signal emissions that could interfere with adjacent avionics.

multipleChoice.question: Why is shielded wire used for sensitive avionics signal wires?
choices: To reduce the weight of the wiring harness by eliminating the need for additional cable supports
To ensure the wire maintains a fixed characteristic impedance regardless of run length
To increase the current-carrying capacity of the conductor by adding a second conducting layer
To prevent external EMI from coupling into the signal wire AND to prevent signal emissions from the wire from interfering with adjacent systems
multipleChoice.explanation: Shielded wire provides bidirectional EMI containment: the shield blocks external fields from disturbing sensitive signals (immunity) and contains signal energy within the cable (emission control).

practice.question: An ARINC 429 bus cable runs near an audio intercom wire. Complaints of HF noise on the intercom and occasional data errors on the ARINC bus are received. What does proper shielding of both cables address?
choices: Shielding both cables prevents ARINC digital transitions from coupling into the audio (emission from ARINC shield) and prevents audio frequency signals from coupling onto the ARINC bus (immunity of ARINC shield)
Shielding only the ARINC bus cable is needed — audio cables don't need shielding
Shielding only the audio cable is needed — digital signals are immune to crosstalk
Neither cable needs shielding if they are separated by at least 3 inches
practice.explanation: Both interference directions occur: ARINC 429 digital transitions radiate into the audio, and audio-frequency noise couples onto the ARINC bus. Proper shielding of both cables resolves both issues.

preassessment.question: What two EMI functions does shielded wire perform?
choices: Increases voltage and reduces weight
Blocks incoming EMI and contains signal emissions
Grounds the circuit and improves power factor
Reduces capacitance and increases impedance

questions.learningObjective: 60/40 rosin-core solder used; acid-core prohibited
questions.fullObjective: State that 60/40 tin-lead or equivalent rosin-core solder is the type typically used in avionics work, and that acid-core solder is prohibited due to corrosive flux residue.

flashcard.definition: Avionics soldering uses 60/40 tin-lead (60% tin, 40% lead) or equivalent rosin-core solder; the rosin flux activates during soldering and leaves a benign residue. Acid-core solder is absolutely prohibited in avionics because its flux is corrosive and will attack copper conductors and connector contacts over time.
flashcard.analogy: Rosin-core solder is like cooking with vinegar that evaporates cleanly — it helps the process without leaving damage. Acid-core solder is like using muriatic acid — it works but destroys everything it touches afterward.

jeopardy.question: In avionics work, 60/40 tin-lead rosin-core solder is standard; this type of solder is strictly prohibited because its flux leaves a corrosive residue that attacks conductors.
choices: Lead-free solder for environmental compliance
Water-soluble flux solder
Silver-bearing solder for high-temperature joints
Acid-core solder
jeopardy.explanation: Acid-core solder contains an acidic flux that remains on the joint after soldering, continuing to corrode copper conductors and contacts. It is absolutely prohibited in avionics work.

multipleChoice.question: What type of solder is used in avionics work, and what type is prohibited?
choices: 60/40 tin-lead or equivalent rosin-core solder is used; acid-core solder is prohibited due to corrosive flux residue
Lead-free solder is required; tin-lead is prohibited due to RoHS regulations
Acid-core solder is preferred for faster flow; rosin-core is prohibited due to high viscosity
Solder is prohibited entirely in avionics — only crimp connections are allowed
multipleChoice.explanation: 60/40 rosin-core solder is the avionics standard. Acid-core solder is strictly prohibited — the acidic flux corrodes copper over time, creating high-resistance joints and eventual open circuits.

practice.question: A technician using a borrowed solder roll notices the label reads 'Acid Core' — typically used for plumbing work. The technician already completed two solder joints on a GPS antenna connection. What must be done?
choices: The joints are acceptable since solder is solder — continue the installation
Remove and redo both solder joints using rosin-core solder, and clean the area with isopropyl alcohol; acid-core flux residue will corrode the copper and contacts if left in place
Apply additional solder on top to seal the acid flux inside the joint
Only redo the joints if they show visible corrosion signs within 24 hours
practice.explanation: Acid-core flux must never be used on avionics. Both joints must be removed immediately; the acid flux left on copper conductors will corrode them over time, creating high-resistance or open connections.

preassessment.question: Why is acid-core solder prohibited in avionics?
choices: It is too expensive for aviation use
It has too high a melting point
It leaves corrosive flux residue that attacks conductors
It creates brittle joints under vibration

questions.learningObjective: Automotive connectors not acceptable in avionics — not FAA-approved materials
questions.fullObjective: Explain why automotive wire connectors are not acceptable for use in an avionics rack: they are not rated for aviation environments and do not meet FAA-approved material standards required by Part 43.

flashcard.definition: Automotive connectors are not approved for aircraft use because they are not rated for aviation temperature extremes, vibration levels, altitude pressure variations, or EMI environments, and do not meet FAA-approved material specifications required under 14 CFR Part 43.
flashcard.analogy: Using an automotive connector in an aircraft is like using a car tire on an airplane — it might physically fit, but it isn't certified for the load, speed, or environment it would encounter.

jeopardy.question: Automotive-grade wire connectors are unacceptable in aircraft avionics racks because they fail to meet this regulatory requirement for aviation maintenance materials.
choices: FAA-approved material standards required under Part 43
ARINC 404 LRU mounting specifications
MIL-SPEC connector size standards
RTCA DO-160 vibration testing specifications
jeopardy.explanation: Part 43 requires that maintenance use materials and parts that meet or exceed FAA-approved standards. Automotive connectors are designed for automotive environments and do not meet aviation temperature, vibration, or altitude specifications.

multipleChoice.question: Why are automotive-grade connectors not acceptable in an aircraft avionics installation?
choices: Automotive connectors cost more than aviation-grade connectors
They are not rated for aviation environments (temperature, vibration, altitude) and do not meet FAA-approved material standards required by Part 43
They have a different pin spacing that prevents mating with aviation LRU connectors
Automotive connectors are acceptable if they are the same physical size as aviation connectors
multipleChoice.explanation: Aviation maintenance must use FAA-approved materials per Part 43. Automotive connectors don't meet aviation temperature ranges (-65°C to +200°C), vibration levels, altitude de-rating, or EMC specifications.

practice.question: During an avionics installation inspection, a DAR finds a crimp splice using an automotive terminal block to join two wires in the avionics bay. Why is this unairworthy?
choices: Automotive terminal blocks are approved if the current rating matches the circuit requirements
The terminal block is only a problem if it is not covered with heat shrink tubing
The automotive terminal does not meet FAA-approved aviation material standards — it is not rated for aviation temperature and vibration environments as required by 14 CFR Part 43
Automotive terminals are acceptable for circuit elements that are not part of IFR systems
practice.explanation: Any connector used in certificated aircraft maintenance must be FAA-approved or meet equivalent aviation standards. Automotive terminals fail to meet aviation environmental requirements and violate Part 43 material standards.

preassessment.question: Why are automotive connectors prohibited in avionics?
choices: Wrong pin size
Wrong color coding
Too expensive
Not FAA-approved for aviation environments per Part 43

questions.learningObjective: Select correct crimping tool and die for connector and wire size
questions.fullObjective: Select the appropriate crimping tool with the correct die for the connector and wire size when replacing a wire connector to ensure a secure and reliable termination.

flashcard.definition: The correct crimp die must match both the terminal/contact type AND the wire gauge being terminated. Using the wrong die produces either an over-crimped (crushed, damaged) or under-crimped (loose, not gas-tight) connection — both of which will fail over time.
flashcard.analogy: Crimp dies are like shoe sizes — the right fit creates a perfect grip. Too big (under-crimp) and the shoe falls off; too small (over-crimp) and it crushes the foot. Only the right size works.

jeopardy.question: When replacing a wire connector, the ratcheting crimp tool must be fitted with a die that matches both the contact type and this, to ensure a correct gas-tight termination.
choices: The wire insulation color code
The wire gauge (AWG size)
The connector housing material
The circuit voltage level
jeopardy.explanation: The crimp die must match both the contact/terminal type AND the wire gauge. The die geometry sets the crimp depth — too shallow (under-crimp) leaves a loose connection; too deep (over-crimp) damages the contact.

multipleChoice.question: When selecting a crimping tool and die for connector replacement, what two specifications must match?
choices: Wire color and connector shell material
The insulation thickness and the connector pin count
The connector/contact type and the wire gauge (AWG size)
The wire run length and the circuit current level
multipleChoice.explanation: Correct crimp tool selection requires matching the die to both the contact type (determines die geometry) and the wire gauge (determines crimp depth). Mismatched dies produce under-crimp or over-crimp failures.

practice.question: A technician is replacing an MS connector on a 22 AWG ARINC 429 bus wire. The crimp tool rack has dies for various sizes. How should the correct die be selected?
choices: Select any die from the same brand as the connector manufacturer
Use the same die that was last used on the bench — leaving it set saves time
Use the largest die available to ensure maximum crimp force
Identify the contact/terminal type from the connector specifications and select the die corresponding to the 22 AWG wire gauge — as shown in the crimp tool manufacturer's die selection chart
practice.explanation: Die selection requires matching the contact type and the wire gauge. The crimp tool manufacturer's die selection chart provides the correct die for each combination — using any other die risks an incorrect crimp.

preassessment.question: What must match when selecting a crimp die for aircraft wire termination?
choices: Contact type and wire gauge
Wire color and circuit voltage
Connector pin count and wire length
Insulation diameter and bundle size

questions.learningObjective: Teflon wire stripping: verify blade depth to avoid nicking conductor
questions.fullObjective: Apply the recommended practice when using automatic wire strippers on Teflon-coated wires: verify the blade depth is set correctly for the wire gauge to avoid nicking the conductor or cutting into the insulation.

flashcard.definition: PTFE (Teflon) insulation is thin and tightly bonded to the conductor. Automatic strippers must have the blade depth precisely set for the wire gauge — too deep nicks the conductor (structural weakness), too shallow leaves insulation residue (insulation contamination of the connection).
flashcard.analogy: Stripping Teflon wire is like peeling a thin-skinned fruit with a sharp knife — set the blade too deep and you cut into the flesh (conductor); too shallow and you leave peel (insulation) behind.

jeopardy.question: When stripping PTFE-insulated wire, automatic stripper blade depth must be set precisely to avoid this defect that weakens the conductor and can cause eventual wire breakage.
choices: Insulation wicking up the stripped area
Oxidizing the copper surface
Nicking the conductor strands
Stretching the insulation beyond the strip length
jeopardy.explanation: Nicking conductor strands weakens the wire at the strip point — a nicked conductor has reduced cross-section and will fail under vibration or bending stress at the nick.

multipleChoice.question: What is the key precaution when using automatic wire strippers on PTFE (Teflon) insulated wire?
choices: Heat the wire first to soften the insulation before using automatic strippers
Apply lubricant to the blade to reduce friction and prevent insulation tearing
Always use manual strippers for Teflon wire — automatic strippers cannot handle PTFE insulation
Verify the blade depth is correctly set for the wire gauge to avoid nicking the conductor strands
multipleChoice.explanation: PTFE insulation is thin — blade depth must be exact. Too deep nicks the conductor; nicked strands create a stress concentration and will break under vibration. Correct blade depth for the gauge is essential.

practice.question: After stripping 20 AWG PTFE wire with an automatic stripper, a technician inspects the stripped end under magnification and sees a groove across one of the outer conductor strands. Is this acceptable?
choices: No — a nicked strand is a structural defect; re-strip at a new location and inspect the strip result before proceeding with termination
Yes — minor nicks in outer strands are within the allowable strand nicking tolerance for all wire gauges
Yes — Teflon wire is designed to be inspected after stripping, and one nicked strand is within tolerance
No — but the nick can be repaired by filling with solder before crimping the contact
practice.explanation: A nicked conductor strand is a defect. The weakened cross-section will fail under vibration. The correct action is to cut back to undamaged wire and re-strip with the correct blade depth.

preassessment.question: What must be verified before stripping Teflon wire with automatic strippers?
choices: Wire insulation color
Correct blade depth for wire gauge
Wire run length
Shield drain wire position

questions.learningObjective: Harness fabrication first step: study the diagram completely before cutting
questions.fullObjective: Apply the correct first step when fabricating a wire harness from a wiring diagram: study the diagram completely to understand all wire runs, connector locations, and pin assignments before cutting any wire.

flashcard.definition: Before cutting any wire when fabricating an aircraft harness, the technician must thoroughly study the wiring diagram to understand all wire runs, connector locations, pin assignments, wire gauges, and routing paths — errors caught on the diagram are free; errors discovered after cutting cost time and materials.
flashcard.analogy: It's the measure-twice-cut-once rule: read the blueprint completely before making a single cut. Understanding the whole plan before acting prevents expensive and time-consuming mistakes.

jeopardy.question: The correct first step when fabricating an aircraft wire harness from a wiring diagram is to study the diagram completely — understanding wire runs, connector locations, and pin assignments — before doing this.
choices: Ordering the connector hardware
Crimping the connector contacts
Labeling the harness breakout points
Cutting any wire
jeopardy.explanation: Studying the diagram first is essential — harness errors discovered after cutting wire waste materials and require re-work. Understanding the complete wire list before cutting is the first and most critical step.

multipleChoice.question: What is the first step when fabricating an aircraft wire harness from a wiring diagram?
choices: Study the wiring diagram completely to understand all wire runs, connector locations, and pin assignments before cutting any wire
Cut all wires to approximate length, then adjust as needed during installation
Install the connectors first, then run wires to each connector as needed
Cut the longest wire first, then use it as a template for subsequent cuts
multipleChoice.explanation: Studying the complete diagram first ensures the technician understands the full harness topology before cutting. Errors on paper are corrected for free; errors in cut wire cost materials and time.

practice.question: A technician is fabricating a 40-wire harness for an avionics upgrade. What should be done before any wire is cut?
choices: Pre-cut all wires to 10% longer than the longest estimated run to allow for adjustments
Review the entire wiring diagram to understand all wire runs, connector pin assignments, gauge requirements, and routing — then create a cut list before cutting anything
Cut one sample wire and route it first to verify the path before cutting all others
Label the connector housings first so they can be laid out before any wires are cut
practice.explanation: Creating a complete understanding of the harness (all runs, gauges, pin assignments) from the diagram before cutting prevents costly errors. A cut list derived from the diagram review ensures all wires are cut correctly the first time.

preassessment.question: What is the first step in aircraft wire harness fabrication?
choices: Cut wires to approximate length
Install connectors first
Study the complete wiring diagram first
Label breakout points

questions.learningObjective: Environmentally sealed splices preferred in exposed areas
questions.fullObjective: State that an environmentally sealed splice (solder-sleeve or adhesive-lined heat-shrink) is the preferred type for wire splices in environmentally sensitive areas such as wheel wells or engine bays.

flashcard.definition: In areas exposed to moisture, fuel, fluids, or temperature extremes (wheel wells, engine bays, bilge areas), wire splices must be environmentally sealed — using a solder-sleeve (pre-tinned solder ring that flows when heated, sealing the conductor) or adhesive-lined heat-shrink over a crimp splice to exclude contaminants.
flashcard.analogy: An environmentally sealed splice is like a waterproof phone case — the phone still works, but it's protected from the hazardous environment around it.

jeopardy.question: In aircraft wheel wells and engine bays where moisture and fluids are present, this type of wire splice is preferred to protect the connection from environmental contamination.
choices: Environmentally sealed splice (solder-sleeve or adhesive-lined heat-shrink)
Open barrel crimp splice
Standard butt splice connector
Tape-wrapped crimp splice
jeopardy.explanation: Environmentally sensitive areas require splices that are sealed against moisture and fluids — solder-sleeve splices or adhesive-lined heat-shrink over crimp splices provide this protection.

multipleChoice.question: What type of wire splice is preferred when making a connection in an aircraft wheel well?
choices: Open crimp butt splice covered with vinyl electrical tape
Environmentally sealed splice — a solder-sleeve type or a crimp splice with adhesive-lined heat-shrink — to exclude moisture and contaminants
Standard uninsulated crimp splice — wheel wells are dry enough for open splices
Direct-solder splice with no covering — solder provides adequate protection
multipleChoice.explanation: Wheel wells are exposed to moisture, de-icing fluid, and dirt. An open splice will corrode quickly. Environmentally sealed splices (solder-sleeve or adhesive-lined heat-shrink crimp) are required for these areas.

practice.question: During a wing repair, a technician needs to splice a landing gear position switch wire inside the wheel well. What type of splice should be used?
choices: A standard crimp butt connector covered with vinyl tape — this is standard practice
A direct solder joint — wheel well temperatures are too variable for heat-activated splices
A solder-sleeve splice or adhesive-lined heat-shrink over a crimp connector — environmentally sealed to prevent moisture and hydraulic fluid from entering the splice
Any available splice since the location is accessible for future inspection
practice.explanation: Wheel wells are exposed to water, hydraulic fluid, and mud contamination. An environmentally sealed splice (solder-sleeve or adhesive-lined heat-shrink) is required to prevent splice corrosion in this area.

preassessment.question: What type of splice is preferred in aircraft wheel wells and engine bays?
choices: Open crimp with vinyl tape
Automotive butt connector
Uninsulated solder joint
Environmentally sealed splice

questions.learningObjective: Wire insulation selection: match temperature, chemical, and abrasion ratings to environment
questions.fullObjective: State that the primary consideration when choosing wire insulation for an aircraft is matching the insulation material's temperature rating, chemical resistance, and abrasion resistance to the installation environment.

flashcard.definition: Wire insulation must be matched to its installation environment on three criteria: temperature rating (must exceed maximum local temperature), chemical resistance (must resist fluids present — fuel, oil, hydraulic fluid), and abrasion resistance (must withstand routing conditions — conduit, structure contact).
flashcard.analogy: Choosing wire insulation is like selecting outdoor clothing — match the material to the weather (temperature), terrain (abrasion), and potential wet conditions (chemical resistance). One-size-fits-all doesn't work.

jeopardy.question: When selecting wire insulation for an aircraft installation, it must be matched to the environment's maximum temperature, potential chemical exposure, and this third factor.
choices: The wire's electromagnetic shielding requirement
Abrasion resistance of the installation area
The wire's minimum bend radius requirement
AWG gauge number compatibility
jeopardy.explanation: The three environmental matching criteria for wire insulation are: temperature rating, chemical resistance, and abrasion resistance. All three must be appropriate for the installation location.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary consideration when selecting wire insulation material for an aircraft installation?
