choices: Selecting the insulation with the lowest dielectric constant to minimize signal loss
Choosing the thinnest insulation available to reduce wire harness diameter
Matching the insulation's temperature rating, chemical resistance, and abrasion resistance to the installation environment
Using the same insulation type throughout the aircraft for maintenance simplicity
multipleChoice.explanation: Insulation must match the environment in three ways: temperature rating (for the local heat), chemical resistance (for fluids present), and abrasion resistance (for routing conditions). Failure in any one of these leads to insulation breakdown.

practice.question: A technician is selecting wire for a replacement circuit in the nacelle of a turbine engine where temperatures reach 170°C and oil mist is present. What insulation properties are most critical?
choices: Low dielectric constant for minimum signal propagation delay
Maximum flexibility for routing around complex engine contours
High insulation thickness to withstand the engine bay vibration
High temperature rating (exceeding 170°C) and resistance to hydrocarbon oil — eliminating PVC and nylon insulations that would melt or swell in this environment
practice.explanation: At 170°C with oil present, the insulation must meet both a high temperature rating (PTFE or fiberglass-rated above 170°C) and resistance to hydrocarbon oil. PVC and nylon insulations would fail in this environment.

preassessment.question: What three properties must wire insulation match for its installation area?
choices: Temperature, chemical resistance, and abrasion resistance
Color, gauge, and length
Capacitance, resistance, and flexibility
Current, voltage, and frequency ratings

questions.learningObjective: Harness routing: supported at intervals, clear of moving parts and heat
questions.fullObjective: Describe how a wire harness should be routed and secured in a small GA aircraft: supported at regular intervals with approved clamps, kept clear of moving parts and heat sources, and routed to avoid sharp bends.

flashcard.definition: A wire harness must be: supported at regular intervals (per manual — typically 12–24 inches) with approved clamps, routed clear of moving parts and heat sources, and have no sharp bends (minimum bend radius = approximately 10× wire diameter for data cables) to prevent insulation damage and conductor fatigue.
flashcard.analogy: A harness should be like a well-organized highway — properly supported (guardrails), free of obstacles (no tight turns), and well-maintained (no contact with hazards).

jeopardy.question: An aircraft wire harness must be supported at regular intervals using approved clamps, kept clear of moving parts and heat, and routed to avoid this type of bend that can damage conductors.
choices: Gradual curves above 90 degrees
Right-angle turns over 45 degrees
Sharp bends below the minimum bend radius
Reverse bends above 180 degrees
jeopardy.explanation: Sharp bends below the minimum bend radius damage insulation, kink conductors, and fracture strands over time. Approved clamps at correct intervals prevent movement and chafing.

multipleChoice.question: How should a wire harness be routed and secured in a small GA aircraft?
choices: Loosely bunched along the fuselage with tie-wraps every few feet
Attached directly to structural members using adhesive foam clips
Secured with electrical tape every 24 inches and routed wherever space is available
Supported at regular intervals with approved clamps, kept clear of moving parts and heat sources, and routed to avoid sharp bends
multipleChoice.explanation: Proper harness installation: approved clamps at regular intervals (prevents sagging and chafing), clearance from moving parts and heat (prevents damage), and no sharp bends (prevents conductor and insulation damage).

practice.question: During an inspection of a newly installed avionics harness, an inspector finds the harness is unsupported over an 18-inch span between clamps, has a tight 90° bend around a bulkhead corner, and runs parallel to a cabin heater duct for 6 inches. Which finding requires immediate correction?
choices: All three: the unsupported span, the tight bend (exceeds minimum bend radius), and the proximity to the heater duct (heat source violation)
Only the 18-inch unsupported span — this exceeds the typical 12-inch maximum
Only the proximity to the heat duct — the other findings are within normal tolerances
Only the tight bend — unsupported spans and heat proximity are acceptable in GA aircraft
practice.explanation: All three are installation defects: unsupported spans can sag and chafe, tight bends damage conductors, and proximity to heat sources degrades insulation. All must be corrected.

preassessment.question: What must be maintained when routing a wire harness in an aircraft?
choices: Only regular clamp spacing
Regular support, clearance from hazards, and no sharp bends
Only clearance from moving parts
Only minimum bend radius

questions.learningObjective: Harness securing methods: lacing cord, nylon tie wraps, cushioned clamps
questions.fullObjective: State that acceptable methods for securing wire harnesses in aircraft include approved MS or AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protectors, or cushioned clamps at intervals specified in the maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: Acceptable aircraft harness securing methods: (1) approved MS/AN lacing cord (traditional military and civilian method), (2) nylon tie wraps with edge protectors where needed, (3) cushioned clamps (AdEL or equivalent) at intervals per the maintenance manual.
flashcard.analogy: Three approved ways to bundle wires — lacing cord is like tying shoelaces, tie wraps are like twist ties, and cushioned clamps are like padded brackets. All must be approved types at correct spacing.

jeopardy.question: Three acceptable methods for securing aircraft wire harnesses are: approved lacing cord, cushioned clamps at specified intervals, and this method with edge protectors where needed.
choices: Automotive spiral wrap
Metal cable trays
Electrical tape wrapping
Nylon tie wraps
jeopardy.explanation: Nylon tie wraps (with edge protection where the harness contacts structure) are an approved harness securing method along with lacing cord and cushioned AdEL-type clamps.

multipleChoice.question: Which methods are acceptable for securing wire harnesses in an aircraft?
choices: Approved MS/AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protection, or cushioned clamps at intervals per the maintenance manual
Rubber bands and cable loops — they avoid contact damage to the outer jacket
Plastic clips from automotive harness kits — these provide adequate support
Electrical tape wrapping at 12-inch intervals — provides both securing and insulation
multipleChoice.explanation: Three approved methods: lacing cord (MS/AN specs), nylon tie wraps (with edge protection), and cushioned clamps per the manual. Automotive hardware, rubber bands, and electrical tape are not approved.

practice.question: A technician is securing a newly fabricated wire harness in a Cessna. The maintenance manual does not specify a specific securing interval in this zone. What securing methods are acceptable?
choices: Electrical tape at 6-inch intervals to keep the harness compact
Approved MS/AN lacing cord, or nylon tie wraps with edge protection where the bundle contacts sharp structure, or cushioned clamps — all at approximately the standard interval
Spiral wrap conduit from the automotive supply store — equivalent protection to aviation clamps
No securing needed if the harness is tensioned by connector placement
practice.explanation: When the manual doesn't specify interval, use standard practice (~12 inches) with approved methods: MS/AN lacing cord, nylon tie wraps with edge protection, or cushioned clamps.

preassessment.question: Which is an acceptable method for securing an aircraft wire harness?
choices: Electrical tape
Rubber bands
Approved nylon tie wraps with edge protection
Automotive spiral wrap

questions.learningObjective: Service loop provides extra wire for future re-termination or repair
questions.fullObjective: Explain the purpose of a service loop in an aircraft wire harness: to provide extra wire length at connectors and terminal points to allow for future re-termination, adjustment, or repair without splicing.

flashcard.definition: A service loop is extra wire length coiled or folded near a connector or terminal, providing material for future re-terminations (each strip-and-crimp operation shortens the wire slightly) — preventing the need for splices when a connector is rebuilt over the aircraft's service life.
flashcard.analogy: A service loop is like leaving slack in a guitar string before tuning — you need extra material to wrap the tuning peg. Without it, you can't tune without splicing more string.

jeopardy.question: The extra wire coiled near a connector in a harness installation is called a service loop; its purpose is to allow re-termination or repair in the future without this costly fix.
choices: Splicing in additional wire
Running an entirely new wire from the equipment
Shortening the connector contact positions
Replacing the entire harness
jeopardy.explanation: Service loops provide wire for future re-terminations. Without a service loop, stripping and re-terminating a wire eventually uses up all the length, requiring a splice to add more wire.

multipleChoice.question: What is the purpose of a service loop in an aircraft wire harness?
choices: To allow the harness to flex during aircraft structural flexing without breaking
To provide extra wire length near connectors for future re-termination or repair without the need for splicing
To distribute the harness weight evenly by creating balanced coils in the bundle
To increase the harness resistance so that each circuit is individually tuned
multipleChoice.explanation: Service loops store extra wire near connectors. Each time a connector is re-terminated (pins replaced, damaged section cut back), the service loop provides material. Without it, the wire becomes too short and a splice must be added.

practice.question: During harness fabrication, a technician skips the service loops at all connector ends to save installation time. What problem will this cause during future maintenance?
choices: The harness will be heavier due to the extra wire coils
Service loops are optional and their omission has no maintenance impact

When connectors need re-termination in the future, there will be no spare wire — each re-crimp shortens the wire slightly, and without service loops, the wire will eventually become too short, requiring a splice
Without service loops, the harness cannot be secured at correct intervals
practice.explanation: Every re-crimp shortens the wire by stripping and trimming back. Without service loops, the wire eventually reaches the routing hardware and can no longer be re-terminated without adding a splice — service loops are a long-term maintainability investment.

preassessment.question: Why are service loops included in aircraft wire harnesses?
choices: To reduce harness weight
To provide electrical filtering
To distribute harness rigidity
To allow future re-termination without splicing

questions.learningObjective: Wire bundles near flight controls: conduit with slack, prevent contact
questions.fullObjective: Apply the best practice for wire bundles routed near moving flight controls: use approved conduit or spiral wrap with sufficient slack, and secure the bundle so it cannot contact or restrict control movement.

flashcard.definition: Wire bundles routed near flight control cables or linkages must be protected with approved conduit or spiral wrap AND have sufficient slack to prevent the bundle from being pulled taut or restricting control movement at any control surface position.
flashcard.analogy: Like giving a leash enough length so it doesn't choke the dog when it turns — the bundle needs enough slack so that full control deflection doesn't stretch, pinch, or break the wires.

jeopardy.question: Wire bundles routed near moving flight controls must be installed with approved conduit or spiral wrap AND this must be provided to prevent restriction of control movement.
choices: Anti-chafe tape
Sufficient slack
Additional ground bonding
Heat shrink tubing
jeopardy.explanation: Slack is essential — a tight bundle near flight controls can be pinched or stretched when the control moves through its full range, risking wire damage or worse, restricting control movement.

multipleChoice.question: What is required when routing a wire bundle near moving flight control linkages?
choices: Route the bundle as close as possible to the controls to minimize length
Attach the bundle directly to the flight control cable to move with it
Use approved conduit or spiral wrap and provide sufficient slack so the bundle cannot contact or restrict control movement at any position
Route the bundle on the opposite side of the fuselage from the flight controls
multipleChoice.explanation: Bundles near flight controls must be protected (conduit/spiral wrap) and have sufficient slack so they cannot bind, pinch, or restrict the controls through their full deflection range — a flight safety requirement.

practice.question: An inspector finds a wire bundle routed parallel to the aileron bellcrank with only 1/8-inch clearance and no conduit protection. At full deflection, the bellcrank touches the bundle. What must be corrected?
choices: Apply heat-shrink tubing over the affected bundle section and add a note to the logbook
The 1/8-inch clearance is acceptable — only direct contact at neutral position is prohibited
Secure the bundle tighter to the structure so it cannot contact the bellcrank

Re-route the bundle with adequate clearance at all control positions, add approved conduit or spiral wrap for protection, and ensure sufficient slack so the bundle cannot contact the bellcrank at any position
practice.explanation: Any wire contact with flight control hardware is a flight safety hazard. Re-routing with adequate clearance, conduit protection, and sufficient slack at all control positions is required.

preassessment.question: What must be provided when routing wire bundles near flight controls?
choices: Conduit or spiral wrap with sufficient slack
Direct attachment to the control cable
Anti-chafe tape only
Maximum tension with zero slack

questions.learningObjective: Chafed wire bundle: remove power, assess damage, follow approved repair procedure
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire bundle damaged by chafing against a metal edge, determine the correct first step: remove power, assess the full extent of damage, and follow approved repair procedures from the maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: When a wire bundle is found chafed by a metal edge: (1) remove all power from affected circuits, (2) assess the full extent of damage (examine the entire contact area, not just the visible portion), (3) follow only approved repair procedures from the applicable maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13.
flashcard.analogy: Discovering a chafed wire is like finding a gas leak — first shut off the valve (remove power), then assess how much pipe is damaged (extent of damage), then use the approved repair method (not duct tape).

jeopardy.question: When a wire bundle is found chafed against a metal edge, the first step is to remove power, then assess the full extent of damage, and then follow only approved procedures from this document.
choices: The aircraft weight and balance report
The aircraft's airworthiness certificate
The applicable maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13
The avionics shop's standard operating procedure
jeopardy.explanation: Approved repair procedures from the maintenance manual or AC 43.13 are the only acceptable methods for repairing chafed wiring. Unapproved repairs render the aircraft unairworthy.

multipleChoice.question: A wire bundle is found chafed against a structural member during an inspection. What is the correct procedure?
choices: Wrap the chafed area with electrical tape and add a note to the next inspection
Apply heat shrink tubing over the chafed section and mark the installation as repaired
Reroute the bundle immediately without removing power to minimize aircraft downtime
Remove power from all affected circuits, assess the full extent of damage, and repair using approved procedures from the maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13
multipleChoice.explanation: Power removal prevents arcing during assessment. Full damage assessment reveals the true extent. Only approved repair procedures (maintenance manual, AC 43.13) ensure the repair meets airworthiness standards.

practice.question: During a pre-flight inspection, a pilot reports a burnt smell near the instrument panel. Investigation reveals a wire bundle has chafed through to bare conductor against a sharp bracket. What are the correct steps in order?
choices: Remove electrical power from all potentially affected circuits, assess the full extent of chafe damage along the entire contact area, and repair per the maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13 — do not return to service until repair is complete and inspected
Apply vinyl tape immediately to prevent further arcing, then assess the full damage
Reroute the bundle past the bracket and restore power to check if the smell is gone
Replace only the visibly damaged wire and restore power for a ground test before flight
practice.explanation: Power removal first (safety), then full damage assessment (not just visible portion), then approved repair only. Returning to service before completing an approved repair is not airworthy.

preassessment.question: What is the first action when discovering a chafed wire bundle?
choices: Apply tape to the damaged area
Remove power from affected circuits
Reroute the bundle immediately
Call the aircraft owner for instructions

questions.learningObjective: Nicked coaxial center conductor: replace the entire cable run
questions.fullObjective: Given a coaxial cable center conductor found nicked, determine the correct action: replace the entire cable run because a nicked center conductor compromises the cable's impedance and structural integrity.

flashcard.definition: A nicked coaxial cable center conductor must result in complete cable replacement — not repair. A nick changes the local impedance (causing signal reflection), reduces mechanical strength (causing fatigue failure), and cannot be reliably repaired to original specification.
flashcard.analogy: A nicked coaxial conductor is like a cracked violin string — you don't patch it, you replace it. Any nick changes the vibration characteristics (impedance) and creates a weak point (fatigue).

jeopardy.question: A nicked coaxial cable center conductor requires complete cable replacement because the nick creates a local impedance change and compromises this property of the conductor.
choices: Electrical resistance rating
Shield coverage percentage
Temperature rating of the insulation
Structural integrity and mechanical strength
jeopardy.explanation: A nick reduces the center conductor cross-section, compromising mechanical strength (vibration fatigue) and changing local impedance (causing signal reflections). Neither is repairable — the run must be replaced.

multipleChoice.question: An avionics technician finds a nick in the center conductor of an NAV/COM antenna coaxial cable during inspection. What is the correct action?
choices: Replace the entire cable run — a nicked center conductor compromises both impedance and mechanical integrity and cannot be reliably repaired
Solder fill the nick to restore conductor cross-section, then continue using the cable
Wrap the nicked area with coaxial repair tape and conduct a VSWR check before approving the cable
Trim the cable back to the nick point and install a coaxial splice connector
multipleChoice.explanation: A nicked coaxial center conductor cannot be reliably repaired. The nick changes local impedance (creating reflections that degrade RF performance) and reduces mechanical strength (promoting fatigue failure). Replace the run.

practice.question: During a pre-installation inspection of a new COM antenna cable, a technician finds a small nick in the center conductor near the mid-span. The cable is otherwise undamaged. Can it be installed?
choices: Yes — the nick is small and the cable will work normally for most frequencies
No — the nicked center conductor compromises impedance and structural integrity; the cable must be replaced with an undamaged one before installation
Yes — apply solder to fill the nick and cover with heat-shrink before installation
Yes — check VSWR after installation; if below 2:1, the cable is acceptable
practice.explanation: A nicked conductor should never be installed. The nick creates an impedance discontinuity that degrades RF performance and is a fatigue failure point. Use an undamaged cable.

preassessment.question: What action is required when a coaxial cable center conductor is found nicked?
choices: Solder fill the nick
Cover with heat shrink and test
Replace the entire cable run
Install a mid-span splice

questions.learningObjective: Damaged harness jacket: assess damage and repair per manual or replace if severe
questions.fullObjective: Given a harness with a damaged outer jacket, determine the correct corrective action: assess the extent of damage and repair using approved heat-shrink or conduit per the maintenance manual, or replace the harness if damage is severe.

flashcard.definition: A damaged harness outer jacket requires a damage assessment: minor/localized damage may be repaired with approved heat-shrink tubing or conduit per the maintenance manual; extensive, widespread, or damage reaching individual wire insulation typically requires harness replacement.
flashcard.analogy: Like assessing a damaged car bumper: a small dent gets repaired; a bumper that has caved into the engine compartment gets replaced. The severity determines the action.

jeopardy.question: When a wire harness outer jacket is damaged, the correct response depends on extent of damage: minor damage allows heat-shrink or conduit repair per the manual, but severe damage requires this.
choices: Harness replacement
Application of vinyl tape over the damaged section
Installation of a metal shield over the damaged area
A one-time logbook notation
jeopardy.explanation: Severe outer jacket damage — especially if individual wire insulation is compromised — cannot be repaired to original airworthiness. The harness must be replaced.

multipleChoice.question: A wire harness outer jacket is found damaged during an inspection. What is the correct approach?
choices: Always replace the entire harness regardless of the extent of damage
Assess the extent of damage: repair with approved heat-shrink or conduit per the maintenance manual for localized damage, or replace the harness if damage is severe or widespread
Apply vinyl tape over any damaged section — this is the standard field repair method
Damage to the outer jacket only is not an airworthiness concern — document and monitor
multipleChoice.explanation: Assessment is the key step. Not all jacket damage requires harness replacement — minor, localized damage may be repaired per the maintenance manual using heat-shrink or conduit. Severe damage requires replacement.

practice.question: Inspection reveals the outer jacket of a wiring harness has been chafed through over a 4-inch section, exposing multiple individual wire insulations. Two wires show visible insulation scoring. What is the correct action?
choices: Repair the outer jacket with heat-shrink tubing and return to service
Wrap the entire section with vinyl electrical tape and note in the logbook

Assess the individual wire insulation damage — with two wires showing scoring, the harness has been compromised beyond acceptable repair limits and replacement is required, not just outer jacket repair
Apply conduit over the section and document as a permanent repair
practice.explanation: When individual wire insulations are damaged (scoring), the harness integrity is compromised beyond what an outer jacket repair addresses. Harness replacement is the correct action when damage reaches individual wires.

preassessment.question: When should a damaged wire harness be replaced rather than repaired?
choices: Never — always repair per the manual
Always replace any harness with outer jacket damage
When outer jacket damage exceeds 1 inch in length
When damage is severe or affects individual wire insulation
